key_insight: A physics-informed residual deep learning model can accurately capture hot deformation and dynamic recrystallization behavior in a Mo-rich titanium alloy while staying consistent with constitutive mechanics.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable constitutive laws for thermomechanical process design and optimization of titanium alloy forging and forming.
method: Trained stacked residual physics-informed neural networks (STAR-PINNs) on experimental flow-stress data.
Enforced thermomechanical constraints such as thermal softening, strain-rate sensitivity, strain hardening, and post-peak softening via automatic differentiation.
Extended the model with DRX-aware dual outputs using JMAK-Avrami regularization and DRX saturation constraints.
Linked predicted recrystallization fraction to stress through latent-feature fusion for physically consistent response prediction.
evidence_signal: The DRX-aware model achieved RMSE = 11.69 MPa, MAE = 4.83 MPa, R² = 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE = 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa across temperatures of 800–1050 °C and strain rates of 0.01–10 s⁻¹.
watch_item: The approach depends on carefully tuned physics constraints and high-quality experimental calibration, so transfer to other alloys or processing windows may require retraining and revalidation.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
